name: shell-integration
description: Load EVM variables into the current shell and manage shell integration
scenario: A user needs EVM variables available in their interactive shell session — not just inside an `evm exec` child process. Demonstrate loading variables via `evm inject` with `eval`, the `evm-load` shortcut, group-targeted injection, secret handling, and key namespacing. Also show how to install/check shell integration with `evm init` and how to silence the first-run auto-install notice in automation.
success_criteria: Uses `eval "$(evm inject)"` to load variables into the current shell (not loadmemory)
Explains that a child process cannot modify the parent shell — inject+eval is the only mechanism
Demonstrates `evm-load` as the shortcut for `eval "$(evm inject)"`
Uses `--group` to inject a specific group (strips the group: prefix)
Notes that grouped vars are skipped by default (invalid shell identifier)
Makes a deliberate decision about `--include-secrets` (decrypt+inject vs skip to avoid leaking ciphertext)
Uses `--prefix` to namespace keys when collisions are a concern
Mentions `EVM_NO_AUTO_INSTALL=1` to silence the first-run notice in automation
Uses `--dry-run` to preview injection without eval-ing
expected_commands: evm init zsh --check   # exit 0 = installed, 1 = not
anti_patterns: Using `evm loadmemory` expecting variables to appear in the parent interactive shell (it only affects the evm process)
Treating grouped variables as directly injectable without `--group` (they're skipped — invalid shell identifier)
Using `--include-secrets` without warning about scrollback/terminal exposure
Forgetting to quote the `eval "$(...)"` (breaks on values with spaces)
